coredata.dc:title: Visible light driven S-scheme GQDs/BiOBr heterojunction with enhanced photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuankun
Zhang, Xinxia
Li, Xing
Zhou, Zhiwei
coredata.dc:description: A crucial and desirable objective in the wastewater treatment field is to design photocatalysts that can efficiently remove emerging pollutants while also being environmental-friendly. This work proposed a novel graphene quantum dots/BiOBr (GQDs/BiOBr) heterojunction with strong interfacial interactions through a hydrothermal method. Consequently, in just 140 min, RhB was almost entirely photodegraded by the GQDs/BiOBr and a rate constant 3.26 times higher than pristine BiOBr. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of GQDs/BiOBr was primarily associated with strengthened visible light absorption and improved charge transfer efficiency, as demonstrated through both experimental characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculation. Furthermore, with only a minor alteration in the surface chemical structure, GQDs/BiOBr retained strong photocatalytic ability after five cycles (RhB degradation of 85.17 %). The construction mechanism of S-scheme heterojunction, photogenerated carriers' transfer and photocatalytic mechanism were deduced by DFT calculation. The results derived from the radical trapping experiments as well as electron spin resonance (ESR) indicated that the presence of h+ and ·O2 − were crucial for the degradation process of RhB. Finally, possible degradation intermediates had been identified and pathways of RhB were proposed, and the results of the toxicity assessment showed that ecotoxicity could be reduced or even eliminated. This effective and reliable visible-light-driven photocatalyst may be applied to build a photocatalytic degradation system for the environmentally friendly and energy-efficient removal of organic pollutants.

(a) XRD patterns of BiOBr and GQDs/BOB-7; High-resolution XPS spectrum of (b) Bi 4f, (c) Br 3d, (d) O 1s and (e) C 1s; (f) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption curve and pore diameter distribution (inset) of BOB and GQDs/BOB-7.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectrum, (b) Corresponding Tauc plots and (c) Mott–Schottky curves of BOB and GQDs/BOB-7; (d) Photocurrent responses curves, (e) EIS spectra and (f) PL spectra (λex = 370 nm) of as-prepared samples.

head.ce:title: Visible light driven S-scheme GQDs/BiOBr heterojunction with enhanced photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Municipal Engineering Department, College of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Municipal Engineering Department, College of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, P. R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A crucial and desirable objective in the wastewater treatment field is to design photocatalysts that can efficiently remove emerging pollutants while also being environmental-friendly. This work proposed a novel graphene quantum dots/BiOBr (GQDs/BiOBr) heterojunction with strong interfacial interactions through a hydrothermal method. Consequently, in just 140 min, RhB was almost entirely photodegraded by the GQDs/BiOBr and a rate constant 3.26 times higher than pristine BiOBr. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of GQDs/BiOBr was primarily associated with strengthened visible light absorption and improved charge transfer efficiency, as demonstrated through both experimental characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculation. Furthermore, with only a minor alteration in the surface chemical structure, GQDs/BiOBr retained strong photocatalytic ability after five cycles (RhB degradation of 85.17 %). The construction mechanism of S-scheme heterojunction, photogenerated carriers' transfer and photocatalytic mechanism were deduced by DFT calculation. The results derived from the radical trapping experiments as well as electron spin resonance (ESR) indicated that the presence of h+ and ·O2 − were crucial for the degradation process of RhB. Finally, possible degradation intermediates had been identified and pathways of RhB were proposed, and the results of the toxicity assessment showed that ecotoxicity could be reduced or even eliminated. This effective and reliable visible-light-driven photocatalyst may be applied to build a photocatalytic degradation system for the environmentally friendly and energy-efficient removal of organic pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial wastewater treatment has become an awkward issue in recent years as the worldwide chemical industry grows and environmental concerns rise. Among various types of industrial wastewater, the treatment of dye wastewater poses a considerable challenge due to its high chromaticity, large volume and poor biochemical properties [1–3]. Furthermore, the pollutants of dye wastewater have been found to contain a wide range of carcinogenic with mercury, chromium, cadmium, lead, and heavy metals. Rhodamine B (RhB), widely used in the dyeing and textile industry, is particularly problematic as its highly stable polymer structure hinders natural decomposition and leads to severe environmental pollution [4,5]. Once the RhB are enriched through the food chain, enter the human body and eventually endanger health. Numerous traditional wastewater treatment methods have been explored, including adsorption [6], membrane separation [7], biological processes [8] and advanced oxidation techniques [9,10]. Despite the application of these techniques in the treatment of wastewater, they are plagued by limitations including suboptimal efficiency and elevated expenses. With a straightforward setup, high efficiency and low energy consumption, photocatalysis, the most prospective advanced oxidation technology, provides a solution for dye wastewater treatment [11,12].

The synthesis of eco-friendly and effective photocatalytic materials is essential for the advancement of photocatalysis technology. In the degradation of dye wastewater, commonly used photocatalysts include metal oxides, metal sulfides, metal-organic frameworks and bismuth-based materials. Among these, bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr), a type of bismuth halide oxide consisting of double slabs of Br atoms interspersed between [Bi2O2]2+ slices, has been widely used for its unique layer structure, superior carrier transport performance and nontoxicity [13,14]. Importantly, BiOBr possesses a suitable band gap (Eg = 2.8 eV), facilitating effective visible light absorption [15,16]. However, because of inherent limitations shared by most semiconductors, including a rapid recombination of electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs and a limited absorption range for visible light, BiOBr alone cannot be used for the photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants [17]. Several methodologies have been applied to modify BiOBr, including the introduction of oxygen vacancies [18,19], elemental doping [20], heterojunction construction with other semiconductors [21,22] and carbon material modification [23,24]. The heterojunction established between BiOBr and other semiconductors is one of these methods that efficiently preserves the high oxidation and reduction potentials of each semiconductor while offering transfer channels for e− and minimizing the recombination of photo-induced carriers [25,26].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of the spherical carbon nanomaterials, exhibit unique photoluminescent properties such as wavelength of excitation dependence and upconversion photoluminescence, enabling effective utilization of the entire solar spectrum [27,28]. With its excellent electron donor and acceptor characteristics [29], GQDs possess numerous functional groups that facilitate rapid electron capture and release, making them a preferred choice among carbon materials for improving photocatalytic performance [30]. Meanwhile, several quantum dot-semiconductor materials, such as GQDs/TiO2 [31], GQDs/Bi2WO6 [32], Bi2MoO6/GQDs/MoS2 [33] and GQDs/g-C3N4 [34], have been successfully prepared and utilized in the photocatalysis field, exhibiting promising results in pollutant degradation. Notably, incorporating GQDs with BiOBr to form heterojunctions for enhancing photoactivity is extremely promising. The considerations are as follows: (1) the upconversion photoluminescence properties of GQDs endow the heterojunction with a wider range of absorption. (2) GQDs can act as electron reservoirs and photoinduced electron transport proteins to facilitate the migration and separation of charge carriers inside BiOBr. Nevertheless, such an outstanding performance heterojunction of GQDs/BiOBr for efficient degradation of RhB has not been explored so far.

Herein, we prepared BiOBr visible light-responsive photocatalysts doped with GQDs and used for the photocatalytic RhB. The specific objectives were: (1) to investigate the influence of GQDs content on the efficiency of GQDs/BiOBr photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants; (2) to examine the factors affecting the photocatalytic degradation of RhB (initial solution pH, pollutant concentration and catalyst dosage); (3) to identify the reactive oxidative species (ROS) within the system and to define the specific reaction sites of these ROS using density functional theory; (4) to determine the intrinsic mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB by electrochemical measurements, work function and DFT calculations; (5) to analyze the potential degradation pathways of RhB and investigate the variations in acute toxicity, mutagenicity and bioaccumulation factors of the intermediates as the photocatalytic process continues. This study reveals a viable solution for efficient RhB degradation and will assist in the development of the photocatalytic-like system.
The used chemicals and reagents were detailed in Text S1 of the Supporting Information.

To obtain GQDs, the procedure involved several steps. Firstly, 100 mg of graphite oxide powder was accurately weighed and dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. The mixture was stirred magnetically at room temperature for 30 min. To this, 0.5 mL of ammonia was added. Subsequently, the homogenous mixture was added to a 100 mL autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled at ambient temperature naturally. Then the product was removed and washed several times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol alternately, centrifuged and filtered. Eventually, the solid product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain GQDs.

The synthesis of the GQDs/BiOBr composite was carried out using a solvothermal approach, as illustrated in Fig. S1. Initially, 6 mmol of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved ultrasonically in a mixture comprising 20 mL of ethylene glycol and 20 mL of glacial acetic acid. To this solution, a specific mass ratio of GQDs to BiOBr (1 %, 3 %, 5 %, 7 %, 10 %) was added and stirred for 30 min at room temperature, forming solution A. Next, 6 mmol of KBr was dissolved in a mixture of 20 mL anhydrous ethanol and 20 mL deionized water, referred to as solution B. Solution B was then added to solution A, and the resulting mixture was continuously stirred magnetically for 30 min. The precursor solutions were subsequently transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and heated at 130 °C for 12 h. Once the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the solid samples were collected. The obtained products were subjected to three consecutive washes using ultrapure water and ethanol. Finally, the samples with varying amounts of GQDs were dried at 60 °C for 24 h. They were denoted as GQDs/BOB-1, GQDs/BOB-3, GQDs/BOB-5, GQDs/BOB-7 and GQDs/BOB-10, corresponding to different GQD percentages. Pristine BiOBr was synthesized following the same procedure, excluding the addition of GQDs and referred to as BOB.
The characterizations of the synthesized materials could be found in Text S2.

A 300 W Xe lamp (CEAULIGHT, CEL-HXF300E7) was placed 20 cm away from the surface of the reaction fluid for photocatalysis. A filter (UVIRCUT400) with a cutoff wavelength of 400 nm was employed to modify the wavelength range to the visible spectrum, specifically from 400 to 780 nm. By using a digital light power meter (CEL-NP2000-2), the light intensity was quantified as 300 mω/cm2. The detailed experimental setup in this work was shown in Fig. S2 and the reaction temperature was kept at 25 °C by circulating water pump. Briefly, 50 mg of photocatalyst was mixed into 200 mL of RhB solution at 10 mg/L and agitated 1 h in the dark to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, turn on the light source and collect the samples at specific intervals. Next, they were processed through a 0.22-μm syringe membrane. The following formula could be determined the RhB degradation rate (DR):

where C is the RhB concentration at a specific reaction time (mg/L), C0 is the preliminary concentration (mg/L) and k is the apparent rate constant for RhB degradation (min−1).

For the comparison experiments, the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr, GQDs/BiOBr, and a physical mixture of “GQDs + BiOBr” were tested respectively using the amount corresponding to that in the GQDs/BiOBr. In the repeatable experiment, GQDs/BOB-7 in the solution after reaction should be removed entirely from the suspension, then washed multiple times using ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol, finally filtered and maintained in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h as the catalysts for the next recurrent experiment. All tests were replicated three times and the average of the results was obtained. Moreover, Text S3 provided sample analysis procedures.
In Text S4 of the Supporting Information, the more thorough DFT calculation procedure was displayed.

The morphology and microstructure of GQDs, BiOBr and GQDs/BiOBr were examined by the utilization of scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). According to Fig. 1(g), GQDs had monodisperse, homogeneous particle size distribution, with an average particle size of 3.01 nm. As shown in Fig. 1(f), the synthesized GQDs showed high crystallinity, and the measured lattice spacing was 0.210 nm, which coincided with the (100) plane of graphite carbon [35]. The irregular nanosheets of BiOBr could be found in Fig. S3(a) and S3(b), exhibiting a high degree of self-assembly to produce 3D flower-like structures. In Fig. 1(a), the sample maintained the nano flower structure because of the tiny particle size of GQDs, which exposed many active sites and effectively shortened the charge transfer distance [36]. Fig. 1(d) and (e) described the TEM results of GQDs/BiOBr. It was discovered that the composites exhibited the morphology of ultrathin nanosheets with GQDs evenly spread on the surface of BiOBr. Furthermore, the distinct lattice spacing of 0.334 nm, 0.282 nm and 0.210 nm in the HRTEM image of GQDs/BOB-7 (Fig. 1(e)) matched the (101) and (102) crystal faces of BiOBr and the (100) lattice plane of GQDs [37], demonstrating the successful bonding of the GQDs with BiOBr. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) images showed that Bi, Br, O and C elements were uniformly spread in the GQDs/BiOBr composite.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to analyze the physical structure of the catalysts. For the pure BiOBr sample, all diffraction peaks were clearly validated for tetragonal phase BiOBr (JCPDS No. 78–0348) [38]. These observations demonstrated that high-purity BiOBr had been successfully prepared. By comparison, no additional diffraction peaks appeared in the composite due to the tiny amount of GQDs. This suggested that the crystallinity of the BiOBr was unaffected by the addition of GQDs. Furthermore, the presence of GQDs in the composites was examined by Fourier-transform Infrared (FT-IR) in Fig. S4. It was determined that the peaks at 1200–1500 cm−1 were responsible for the BrBi bond [39], while the apparent peak at 1614 cm−1 matched the OH bending vibration bond, possibly due to the adsorbed water. The peak at 2360 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration CO bond [40]. In addition, as shown in Fig. S4 (b), defective oxygen at 1076 cm−1 was found in BiOBr while it shifted toward lower wavelengths to 1055 cm−1 in GQDs/BOB-7 composite, suggesting a significant interaction between GQD and BiOBr [41].

The surface structure of the sample was further analyzed via Raman spectroscopy. Fig. S5 showed the Raman spectra of GQDs, BiOBr and GQDs/BiOBr. The bands located at 71 cm−1 and 125 cm−1 for pristine BiOBr can be severally ascribed to the A 1 g external and A 1 g interior BiBr stretching mode [42]. In addition, the peak at 390 cm−1 belonged to the stretching, bending and rocking modes of BiO [27]. The peaks of the composites were shifted with respect to BiOBr, while the G-band (1598 cm−1) and D-band (1323 cm−1) of carbon can be clearly observed, which suggested the presence of GQDs in the composites, and a strong interaction between GQDs and BiOBr.

The chemical state and the direction of electron transfer at the interface of materials were further characterized using X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. S6 presented the full XPS survey scan, which confirmed the presence of Bi, O, Br and C elements in GQDs/BiOBr. Fig. 2(b) was shown the Bi 4f XPS spectra of BiOBr, and it possessed two distinctive peaks with binding energy was 164.5 and 159.1 eV, where the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 spin-orbit components of Bi3+ [43]. In GQDs/BOB-7, the diffraction peaks appeared at 164.3 and 158.9 eV, with the peak shifted toward the reduced binding energy. The same phenomenon was found in Br 3d spectrum in Fig. 2(c), implying that the electron density in BiOBr increased through the formation of fantastic heterojunction [44]. Two peaks in the O 1 s of GQDs/BOB-7 were visible in Fig. 2(d), and it was considered that the peak at 529.78 eV corresponded to BiO bond, while another peak was assigned to the oxygen vacancy [45,46]. In addition, the C 1s spectrum of GQDs in Fig. 2(e) displayed four peaks located at 284.15, 284.80, 285.95 and 287.11 eV, coinciding with CCa (amorphous carbon), C=C/C-C, C-OH and O=C-HO [47–49]. However, the peaks in composites were shifted toward the increased binding energy. The peak located at 288.54 eV belongs to the Bi-O-C bond, which indicated the intense electronic coupling between GQDs and BiOBr constituents. Besides, the complex GQDs/BOB-7 acquires oxygen-containing groups such as CO and -OH from GQDs. The existence of these groups aided in the formation of stable interfacial heterojunction and demonstrated the enormous potential of GQDs/BiOBr as an effective photocatalyst.

The surface area of Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (SBET) and the pore structure of the catalysts were also analyzed. The BOB and GQDs/BOB-7 all exhibited typical type II isotherms with an H3 hysteresis loop in Fig. 2(f), indicating a mesoporous and large pores structure, which was attributed to the stacking of nanosheets [50]. Notably, Table S1 showed that the SBET of GQDs/BOB (27.61 m2/g) was 2.76 times more than pristine BOB (10.02 m2/g). Besides, the average pore sizes were respectively 15.12 nm and 9.71 nm. In general, higher SBET and mesoporous characteristics represent the availability of more reaction sites [51], which enhances the O2 adsorption ability and thus generates more ROS and improves the photocatalytic performance.

The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (UV–vis DRS) were applied to measure the optical characteristics of photocatalysts. Fig. 3(a) presented that introducing GQDs into BiOBr increased the absorption edge. It was attributed to the two-photon or multi-photon reactions in GQDs that could excite shorter wavelengths, thus promoting visible light absorption [52]. The Eg of the samples as-obtained were derived in Fig. 3(b) using the Tauc method. Therefore, GQDs/BiOBr (2.73 eV) had a lower Eg than pure BiOBr (2.82 eV), which might increase the photodegradation rate and better utilize visible light.

The Mott-Schottky plot of the samples was presented in Fig. 3(c). GQDs/BiOBr and BiOBr exhibited flat band potentials (EFB) −0.44 eV vs SCE (−0.20 vs NHE) and −0.50 eV vs SCE (−0.26 vs NHE), respectively. In addition, the conduction band potential (ECB) was usually 0.1–0.3 eV less than the EFB for n-type semiconductors [53], and the EFB and ECB were distinguished by a voltage of 0.2 eV. Therefore, the final ECB values of GQDs/BiOBr and BiOBr were −0.40 eV (vs NHE) as well as −0.46 eV (vs NHE) for each, certifying the CB electrons owned powerful reduction capability to convert O2 to ·O2 −. Furthermore, following the equation (Eg = EVB - ECB), the VB energy position (EVB) values were determined as 2.33 and 2.36 eV, evidencing that the VB holes were unable to create ·OH.

In general, it was critical for the e−-h+ pairs produced during photocatalysis to separate and migrate efficiently. Therefore, the photovoltaic properties were evaluated by transient photocurrent (I-t), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and spectroscopy linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). As shown in Fig. 3(d), the GQDs/BOB-7 photocurrent density was much higher compared to pure BiOBr, which was also consistent with the LSV curve results (Fig. S7), indicating that the composite could separate the e−-h+ pairs most efficiently. In addition to this, EIS was also used to investigate its charge transfer capability. EIS plots in Fig. 3(e) showed that GQDs/BOB-7 had the smallest arc radius, indicating it had a relatively smaller charge transfer resistance which would provide enhanced separation and fast transport of electrons and holes. Fig. 3(f) presented the PL spectrum, where the GQDs/BOB-7 peak exhibited the lowest intensity. This observation suggested the lowest rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs complexation of GQDs/BOB-7 composite. In summary, the photoelectric performance assessment revealed that the formed heterojunction significantly enhanced the separation of carriers, resulting in better photocatalytic capability of the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation of energy band structure
ce:section.ce:para: DFT calculation was used further to explore energy band structures of the as-obtained samples. Fig. 4(a) demonstrated that the Eg of BiOBr was 2.57 eV, which was in agreement with previous studies [45] and close to the experimental value. Additionally, the energy band diagram of BiOBr showed that the valence band top (VBT) and conduction band bottom (CBB) were found at distinct positions, further demonstrating its typical indirect band gap semiconductor properties [54]. The band structure of GQDs/BOB showed a discrepancy between the theoretical calculation and experimented conclusions that the computed Eg was extremely unremarkable, which might be attributable to a shortcoming in the DFT approach [55]. Additionally, a higher peak value in the related density of states diagram existed when the line was more intensive in the band structure drawing, suggesting the presence of more electrons [56]. The energy band structure diagram of the composite had a denser curve in Fig. 4(b). More electrons could generate extra carriers during the photocatalytic process, which might contribute to improved photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the orbital states of the electrons in the catalysts were explicitly disclosed by the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS). In Fig. 4(c), the VBT of BiOBr consisted of both s and p orbitals of Bi, Br and O hybridized, whereas the CBB was mainly derived from hybridized Bi 6s, Bi 6p, Br 4s and O 2s. The PDOS of GQDs/BOB showed that its VBT consists of s and p orbitals of four elements (C, O, Br, Bi), and the corresponding CBB consisted of Bi 6s, Bi 6p, Br 4s, C 2p and O 2p. Surprisingly, the VBT of GQDs/BOB shifted favorably, presumably because the orbitals hybridized and subsequently narrowed the band gap [56]. This redistribution of electrons contributed to the isolation from photogenerated carriers and thus improved photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic activity of the materials was examined in the presence of visible light. Without the addition of photocatalysts, there was no decrease in the RhB concentration. As shown in Fig. S8, the adsorption-desorption equilibrium could be reached about 60 min and the adsorption of RhB on pure BiOBr and GQDs/BiOBr was less than 10 % in dark condition. The degradation rate of RhB through pure BiOBr could reach about 83 % after 140 min, but the TOC removal rate was only 11.22 %. The removal rates of both RhB and TOC by GQDs/BOB were significantly increased. With a 99.99 % photodegradation efficiency of RhB in just 140 min, the GQDs/BOB-7 sample outperformed all other photocatalysts. Interestingly, the photocatalytic ability of the GQDs/BiOBr decreased as the GQDs component was increased further because excessive GQDs overlayed the catalytic reactive sites and shielded the visible light absorption [48]. However, when a mixture of GQDs with BiOBr at a mass ratio of 7 % was added to the system, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB reached 83.1 % within 140 min, with a photocatalytic rate of only 0.0137 min−1, which was not much different from the catalytic effect produced by the addition of the same amount of BiOBr. More importantly, GQDs/BOB-7 showed 32.01 % removal of TOC, exhibiting a better mineralization rate (2.85 times higher than pure BiOBr). The k value of GQDs/BOB-7 was 3.26 times more than that of BiOBr (Fig. 5(b)). According to the experimental results (Fig. S9) and considering the cost, GQDs/BOB-7 was chosen as the catalyst, RhB concentration and dosing amount were 10 mg/L and 0.25 g/L as the following experimental conditions. Furthermore, the GQDs/BOB-7 synthesized in this work were more effective for the degradation of RhB than previously proposed catalysts (Table S2), demonstrating its potential for degrading dye wastewater.

Generally speaking, the impact of pH on photocatalytic systems is not negligible. We evaluated the degradation effect of GQDs/BOB-7 on RhB at various initial pH. In Fig. 5(d), both acidic and alkaline conditions were not favorable for the degradation of RhB. It was due to the excess H+ and OH− will scavenge the ROS within the system or generating almost inactive radicals, thus reducing the photocatalytic activity.

Moreover, we investigated the presence of typical anions Cl−, NO3 − and CO3 2− in water on the catalytic efficiency of GQDs/BOB-7. Fig. 5(e) demonstrated that the impact of Cl− and NO3 − on the photocatalytic process was minor since they cannot hinder the RhB from reacting with the active sites [48]. Moreover, CO3 2− had a more significant effect on the photocatalytic system. It was due to the hydrolysis of CO3 2− to generate OH− and HCO3 − (Eq. (6)) [57], and the alkaline environment created by the extra OH− will make the degradation rate of RhB slowly, while CO3 2− and the free radicals will react in the system to produce less reactive free radicals (Eq. (7)) [58,59]. Consequently, the photocatalytic degradation effect gradually diminished with increased CO3 2− concentration in the system.

Humic acid (HA) is an important component of natural organic matter and is typically found in surface water and wastewater treatment plants at levels ranging from 0.1 to 10 mg/L [60]. Therefore, it is also important to study the effect of different concentrations of HA on the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. As shown in Fig. 5(f), the performance of photocatalytic degradation of RhB decreased with increasing HA concentration. When the concentration was increased to 5 mg/L, the degradation of RhB by the photocatalytic system was reduced to 89.7 %, which was about 10 % less compared with the control experiment without HA addition. The decrease in photocatalytic efficiency may be due to the competition between HA and RhB for the active species in the system [61]. However, in any case, the degradation of RhB by the photocatalytic system was maintained at about 90 % at a higher HA concentration (5 mg/L).

In order to further investigate the degradation ability of the GQDs/BiOBr photocatalytic system on different pollutants, common dyes (Rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), Congo red (CR)) and antibiotics ((ciprofloxacin (CIP), sulfamethazine (SMT), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl)) were selected as pollutants with a concentration of 10 mg/L for photocatalytic experiments. As shown in Fig. 5(g), the removal rate of all pollutants exceeded 80 %. Meanwhile, it can be seen that the photocatalytic process was well fitted with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model in Fig. 5(h). Therefore, the GQDs/BiOBr photocatalytic system can effectively degrade different types of pollutants, indicating a high degree of applicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic stability and reusability of GQDs/BOB-7 were evaluated in order to assess the promising application. In Fig. 5(i), the photocatalytic activity of RhB by GQDs/BOB-7 remained above 85 % after five cycles. The occupation of active sites during the recycling and regeneration process may have contributed to the decline in degradation efficiency [62]. The XRD patterns of the GQDs/BOB-7 composites showed a striking stability with little noticeable changes before and after undergoing five cycles of degradation to RhB (Fig. S10(a)). The accompanying Fig. S10(b)-(f) further showed that after five cycles, the XPS patterns of the GQDs/BOB-7 composites were still clearly visible for the Bi 4f, Br 3d, C 1s, and O 1s peaks, and the positions of these typical peaks remained almost unchanged. SEM images of the materials before and after photocatalytic degradation of RhB did not show significant differences, and the four elements were still uniformly distributed on the surface of the materials (Fig. S11(a)-(c)). In addition, the N2 adsorption-desorption curves of the materials after five cycles showed that the specific surface area of the materials was almost slightly reduced (Fig. S11(d)). In Fig. S11(e), the Raman spectra of the used materials are essentially identical in position and intensity to the original spectra. Besides, FT-IR patterns in Fig. S11(f) suggested that there was no significant change in the surface specific groups of GQDs/BOB-7 before and after photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, GQDs/BiOBr had an excellent recyclability and stability and could potentially treat actual dye wastewater.

By the surface work function (Φ) obtained from theoretical calculation, the charge transfer direction at the heterogeneous contact was examined. Fig. 6(a) and (b) showed that the Φ values of BiOBr and GQDs were 5.425 and 4.873 eV, and the Φ of GQDs was lower, while Fermi energy (EF) was higher than BiOBr. This meant that after the heterojunction of GQDs and BiOBr, e− will spontaneously transfer from GQDs to BiOBr until the EF reaches equilibrium. Additionally, the loss of e− will result in higher-bent energy band edges for GQDs. In contrast, the aggregation of e− caused the energy band margins of BiOBr to be bent downward. As a result of the charge redistribution and powerful chemical interactions at the interface, negatively charged BiOBr and positively charged GQDs could generate an internal electric field (IEF) [62].

In order to acquire the ROS formed during the RhB photodegradation process, scavenger experiments were carried out. For scavengers of radicals ·O2 −, h+ and ·OH, respectively, p-benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) and isopropanol (IPA) with a concentration of 5 mM were utilized. The degradation rate of RhB reduced from 99.99 % to 71.23 % and 12.60 %, respectively, when BQ and EDTA-2Na were added, as shown in Fig. 6(c), but just a minor reduction (96.99 %) was observed when IPA was present. Thus, ·O2 − and h+ constituted the main ROS for RhB degradation.

To further recognize the ROS in the reaction process, ESR was applied. To detect the presence of h+ under visible light, the TEMPO, as a spin-trapping agent, was utilized for radicals. We could see that the h+ signal in the system declined after 15 min of light exposure in Fig. 6(d), suggesting h+ present in the system. On the other hand, DMPO was employed as a trapping agent for the detection of ·O2 − and ·OH. Here, the standard peak of ·O2 − was significant, whereas the ·OH signal was barely noticeable. A combination of scavenger experimental results and ESR demonstrated that both h+ and ·O2 − were crucial to the photocatalytic system.

According to DFT calculations and observations, particularly the formation of S-scheme heterojunction at the interface, the photocatalytic mechanism of GQDs/BiOBr was proposed (Fig. 7 ). Both BiOBr and GQDs could produce photogenerated e− and h+ under visible light (Eqs. (8) and (9)). The upconversion luminescence property of GQDs could modify long-wavelength light to short-wavelength light, so it could also excite the e− at the VB position to the CB in BiOBr. After the contact between GQDs and BiOBr, the resulting energy band bending due to the formation of IEF promoted the complexation of e− in the position of CB in BiOBr and h+ in the VB of GQDs at the interface (Eq. (10)). Therefore, the h+ in the VB of BiOBr and the e− in the CB of GQDs could exhibit high redox properties. Moreover, e− might combine with oxygen in the aqueous environment to form ·O2 − (Eq. (11)). However, the energy band bending resulted in EVB being somewhat lower than the potential of H2O/·OH (+2.27 eV vs NHE), demonstrating that it was challenging for the h+ to react with H2O to generate ·OH. In addition, h+ itself was ROS that could attack RhB directly. Finally, h+ and ·O2 − oxidize RhB to small molecules, CO2 and H2O (Eq. (12)). In summary, the possible mechanism of the visible light degradation process was as follows:
(8)
BiOBr + hv → BiOBr
e CB −
h VB +
(9)
GQDs + hv → GQDs
e CB −
h VB +
(10)
e CB −
BiOBr
+
h VB +
GQDs
→ S − scheme recombination
(11)
e CB −
GQDs
+
O 2
→ ⋅
O 2 −
(12)
h VB +
BiOBr
+ ⋅
O 2 −
+ ⋅ OH + RhB → small molecules +
CO 2
+
H 2
O

By using 3D EEMs technology, the dynamic oxidation of RhB was monitored at various photocatalytic intervals. The results of the samples collected at different intervals were shown in Fig. S12. RhB was gradually decomposed to N-deethylation intermediates in the pre-photocatalytic stage, but the concentration of RhB was higher, so a decrease in fluorescence intensity appeared [63]. However, as the photocatalytic process continued, the yield of the intermediates increased, and the phenomenon of blue shift and fluorescence intensity grew. After that, the intensity of the fluorescence peak steadily diminished, revealing that the generated N-deethylation intermediates could be further decomposed into small molecules [64]. Consequently, the GQDs/BiOBr composites not only decolorized RhB but were dominated by further mineralization of RhB macromolecular intermediates.

To clarify the reaction sites of RhB, DFT calculations were carried out. Fig. 8(b) showed the electrostatic surface potential (ESP) distribution of RhB. Fig. 8(d) and (e) showed the frontier molecular orbitals (the highest occupied molecular orbits (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbits (LUMO)) of the optimized RhB molecule, and it could be seen that the HOMO of RhB was mainly concentrated near N2 and C4. However, ESP, HOMO and LUMO were unable to pinpoint the attack site of ROS precisely, so Fukui index was utilized on RhB [65]. It is generally believed that ·O2 − is a strong nucleophilic reagent that reacts rapidly with organic substances through nucleophilic substitution [66]. O2 molecules and h+ are considered to be electrophilic substances. According to the radical capture experiments and ESR results, h+ and ·O2 − were the ROS responsible for the oxidation of RhB. Therefore, their reactive sites must be further determined based on the Fukui index (f−, f+ and f0) of RhB [67]. The specific values of Fukui index for all atoms in the optimized RhB were shown in Table S3. According to Fig. 8(c), N2, C7, N14 and C19 were electrophilic, while C20, C23, C24, C25 and C26 were nucleophilic. Furthermore, although C7, C19, C20 and C26 exhibited strong electrophilicity and nucleophilicity, respectively, they were difficult to be attacked by ROS due to the saturation site and spatial site resistance [68]. Therefore, we concluded that N2, N14, C23, C24 and C25 of RhB were highly vulnerable to ROS in the photocatalytic system.

The results of LC-MS further elucidated the intermediates produced during RhB degradation and the mass spectra at different times were shown in Fig. S13. Table S4 was listed relevant information of detected intermediates. Combining the results of DFT calculations and ROS analysis, Fig. 8(f) presented the potential degradation pathways of RhB in the photocatalytic process. Apparently, RhB was degraded to small molecules as well as CO2 and H2O through steps such as N-deethylation, deamination, chromophore cracking, ring opening and mineralization [69]. In pathway I, due to the high f− and f0 of N14, the RhB molecule will systematically attack N14 for N-deethylation by the action of electrophilic (h+) and radical substances (·OH), but ·OH was not present in the system according to ESR and radical trapping experiments. Thus, the RhB molecule was only attacked by the electrophilic substance h+ to generate P1 (m/z = 415). After that, the N, N-diethylrhodamine (P2, m/z = 387) was generated by the P1 removing an ethyl group. Then, P2 experienced further de-ethylation to produce P3 (m/z = 359) and P4 (m/z = 331). Subsequently, P4 was further oxidized by ROS through a deamidation process to form P5 (m/z = 315). In pathway II, h+ directly attacked N14 to produce P6 (m/z = 374), which then underwent the same demethylation step as in pathway I to form P7 (m/z = 318). P5 generated in pathway I and P7 generated by pathway II were stripped of imino and amino groups to generate P8 (m/z = 302), respectively. Because C23 had high f+, P8 changes to P9 (m/z = 182) in the presence of nucleophilic substance (·O2 −), and then P9 produced P10 (m/z = 166), P11 (m/z = 138) and P12 (m/z = 110). Currently, C7, C19, C20 and C26 were no longer affected by saturation sites and spatial site resistance, having high electrophilic offensive properties, which could further open the ring to generate P13-P17 in the presence of h+. Ultimately, the small intermediates were further mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

Using the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.), the acute toxicity, mutagenicity and bioaccumulation factors of RhB and its degradation intermediates were evaluated in accordance with the quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR). Notably, the specific values were displayed in Table S5. Acute toxicity was defined using the LC50 of daphnia magna and the LD50 of oral rats. RhB was identified to be “toxic” to daphnia magna (LC50 = 6.21 mg/L) and “very toxic” to oral rats (LD50 = 545.25 mg/kg). Additionally, the LC50 of all degradation products except for P9 was higher than that of RhB. Notably, P9 was totally decomposed to P10, P11 and P12 according to the result of LC-MS (Fig. S13). Also, most RhB products had higher LD50 values for rats than for RhB. In particular, the LD50 values of final degradation P13 and P14 significantly increased, indicating the formation of low-toxic compounds as the final stage. As shown in Fig. 9(c), most intermediates became mutagenic negative compared to RhB (mutagenic positive), suggesting that photocatalytic system reduced the mutagenicity of RhB. Moreover, the bioaccumulation factor of further degradation products was less than that with RhB in Fig. 9(d), indicating a reduced bioaccumulation effect in the GQDs/BiOBr photocatalytic system. Overall, the photocatalytic process of RhB by GQDs/BiOBr is a progressive detoxification process and further mineralization of these intermediates can improve the safety of the ecosystem.

In this study, a novel S-scheme GQDs/BiOBr photocatalyst was successfully constructed. According to the photocatalytic degradation, nearly all RhB was removed by GQDs/BOB-7 within 140 min under a preliminary concentration of 10 mg/L. Furthermore, the removal rate of TOC reached 32.01 %, which was 2.85 times higher than pure BiOBr (11.22 %). GQDs/BiOBr retained excellent reusability and stability after five cycles of reuse. According to DFT calculations, the separation of carrier was promoted by the formation of S-scheme GQDs/BiOBr heterojunction, which lead to strong oxidation and reduction properties of carriers. The dynamic degradation process of RhB was followed by 3D EEMs, which confirmed that the elimination of RhB through the GQDs/BiOBr photocatalytic system was not only decolorized but also dominated by further mineralization of intermediate products. h+ and ·O2 − were confirmed to be the primary active substances toward the RhB degradation in the photocatalytic process. The Fukui index estimation suggested that the greater contribution rate of h+ to RhB degradation may be due to its electrophilic attack activity. In addition, two potential photocatalytic degradation pathways of RhB were proposed. Finally, the degradation intermediates and final products have been proven to have lower toxicity than RhB with QSAR analysis. This work offers valuable information into the preparation of novel, highly effective heterogeneous structured photocatalysts and their prospective applications in environmental remediation.
